titleStmt.title: Chiral pair density wave states generated by spin supercurrents

affiliation.orgName: Department of Physics and Nanoscience Center
Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology
Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology
Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology
National Research University Higher School of Economics

analytic.title: Chiral pair density wave states generated by spin supercurrents

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We report that spin supercurrents in magnetic superconductors and superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator bilayers can induce the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction which strength is proportional to the superconducting order parameter amplitude. This effect leads to the existence of inhomogeneous parity-breaking ground states combining the chiral magnetic helix and the pair density wave orders. The formation of such states takes place via the penetration of chiral domain walls at the threshold temperature below the superconducting transition. We find regimes with both the single and the re-entrant transitions into the inhomogeneous states with decreasing temperature. The predicted hybrid chiral states can be found in the existing structures with realistic parameters and materials combinations.
To start with let's demonstrate that the DMI interaction of the form (1) is induced by spin supercurrents.

Recently the interest in superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator (S/FI) structures has been growing rapidly. Experimental advances in fabrication of S/FI systems has resulted in the observation of the spin-split spectrum of Bogolubov quasiparticles in Al/EuS systems [1][2][3][4] . The combination of the effective Zeeman field generated by the magnetic proximity effect and superconductivity leads to the long-range spin transport in EuS/Al systems [5][6][7][8] , non-trivial spin pumping effects in NbN/GdN 9 and Nb/yttrium iron garnet (YIG) 10 structures. Moreover recently the InAs/Al/EuS multilayers combining superconductivity, ferromagnetism and spin-orbit coupling (SOC) have been proposed as the platforms for the realization of the topological superconductivity 11,12 .

In this Letter we demonstrate that the interplay of superconductivity, ferromagnetism and SOC generates a non-trivial ground state characterized by the chiral spin order accompanied by the spatial modulation of the superconducting order parameter. Such state which we call the chiral pair density wave (CPDW) state can occur in S/FI systems with interfacial SOC induced by heavy metal Pt layers as shown in Fig. 1 as well as in a wider class of magnetic superconductors with spin-singlet order parameter [13][14][15][16] .

The CPDW states break the parity symmetry and have several other qualitatively different features compared to the previously studied cryptoferromagnetic states 17 in the weakly magnetic superconductors 18 and ultra-thin metallic ferromagnetic films on top of the superconductor 19,20 .

The mechanism which provides the formation of CPDW state is the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction [21][22][23] (DMI) generated by the superconducting spin currents. The corresponding DMI interaction energy has the form
div.formula: F DM = |ψ| 2 d k • (m × ∇ k m)(1)
div.p: where m = m(r) is the magnetic texture, d 1,2,3 are the DMI vectors and ψ is the s-wave spin-singlet superconducting order parameter. The interaction between the superconductivity and magnetism provided by Eq.( 1) is determined by the magnetization gradients. Therefore it is conceptually distinct from the mechanism which involves superconducting phase gradients discussed in the context of the Edelstien effect [24][25][26][27] , helical superconducting states 28,29 , spontaneous currents 30,31 and the anomalous Josephson effect 32 .

The DMI provides the formation of magnetic spirals and skyrmions 33,34 which are considered as the building blocs of the future spintronics devices 35 . Therefore controlling DMI with external parameters such as the voltage and strain attracts significant attention [36][37][38][39] . The superconductivity-induced DMI (1) provides a plethora of different effects resulting in the control over the magnetic textures with the help of the superconducting correlations.

FIG. 1. Geometry of the multilayered material consisting of S film in contact with heavy metal Pt layer having interfacial Rashba-type SOC vector n and FI inducing the Zeeman field in S. There is a superconducting spin current J γ j in S which magnitude and direction depends of the direction of the magnetic moment m. The spin torque induced by the gradients of J γ j leads to the DMI (see text).

Consider a spin-singlet superconductor with the Zeeman field h(r) = hm(r), where |m| = 1 and the linear in momentum SOC potential V so = α SO A γ k σγ p k . Here σ and p are the electron spin and momentum, α SO is the SOC strength and A γ k σγ is the global SU(2) field. E.g. for the Rashba-type SOC A γ k = n j ε jγk , where n is the anisotropy vector.
Our first main result is that the Ginzburg-Landau free energy of this system can be written as follows 40
div.formula: F S = aψ 2 + b 2 ψ 4 + c(∇ψ) 2 - a m 2 (D k m) 2 ψ 2(2)
div.p: Coefficients a, b, c determine the usual GL free energy in a diffusive superconductor with the Zeeman splitting. The last term in (2) provides the coupling between superconductivity and ferromagnetism determined by the covariant derivative of the magnetization 41,42
div.formula: D k m = (∇ k -α SO A k ×)m, where A k = (A x k , A y k , A z k ). The co- efficient is a m = πνDT 3 c h 2 ω>0 (h 2 + ω 2 ) -2
div.p: , where ω are the Matsubara frequencies. By the order of magnitude a m ∼ νT 2 c ξ 2 0 (h/T c ) 2 , where T c is the superconducting critical temperature and ν is the normal state density of states in the superconductor. The physical meaning of this term is the twisted exchange interaction between spins mediated by the Cooper pairs in S. It generalizes seminal de Gennes result 43 on the renormalization of RKKY interactions in superconductors to the case when the SOC is present. Quite importantly, a m > 0 so that the gradients of m become less energetically costly. In the absence of SOC this leads eventually to the negative spin stiffness resulting in the cryptoferromagnetic state 17,20 . In the presence of SOC the situation is more complicated because as we show below the CPDW state appears even for the overall positive spin stiffness.

The last term in Eq. ( 2) can be interpreted as the energy of spin supercurrent. Indeed using the Usadel equation and the recently obtained general quasiclassical expression for the superconducting free energy 44 we obtain the general expression for the equilibrium spin current in the superconductor with spin-singlet pairing 40
div.formula: J γ k = δ δA γ k drF S(3)
div.p: The equilibrium spin supercurrent Eq. ( 3) provides a field-like spin torque acting on the magnetic texture m(r). The correction to the effective field from superconducting correlations is δH ef f = -δF S /δM . The corresponding spin torque is given by T = γm × δH ef f and can be written as 45
div.formula: T = γD k J k(4)
div.p: where J k = (J x k , J y k , J z k ). This expression agrees with the general symmetry of a gauged Heisenberg model under the local spin rotations 41,42 .
The covariant magnetic energy in Eq. ( 2) has three qualitatively different contributions (
div.formula: D k m) 2 = (∇ k m) 2 + α 2 SO (A k × m) 2 -2α SO A k (m × ∇ k m).
div.p: The first two terms provide renormalization of the usual exchange 20,43,46 and anisotropy energies which are present already in the free energy of the ferromagnet
div.formula: F F I = [A(∇m) 2 + Km 2 z ]/2.
div.p: Here we assume the K can have arbitrary sign, so that in the normal state the structure is in-plane (out-of plane) magnetized m x (m z). As we show below the superconducting spin current energy in (2) typically yields the easy-axis anisotropy correction which can overcome the intrinsic easy-plane one in case is K > 0 and lead to the magnetic reorientation transition. The change of anisotropy in the superconducting state of S/FI systems has been recently observed experimentally [47][48][49][50] . The third term is the DMI in the form (1) with vectors
div.formula: d k = 2a m α SO A k . (5
div.p: This DMI appears only in the superconducting state with SOC because of the non-local twisted spin response mediated by the condensate. The non-local character of the DMI in the considered system provides the chiral coupling between the order parameter and magnetization which has not been taken into account in the previous works on the ferromagnetic superconductivity.

In order to feature such an interaction the system should have three basic ingredients: usual spin-singlet superconducting pairing, exchange field and SOC. The generic device of such type is shown in Fig. 1. It consists of the usual S layer sandwiched between the FI and the heavy metal Pt films. The S thickness d S is much larger than the atomic scale but smaller than the superconducting coherence length ξ 0 = D/T c , where D is the diffusion coefficient in S.

The exchange field in S is induced in result of the spin-mixing scattering of electrons at S/FI interface [1][2][3][4]11,12,[51][52][53] . For thin S film d S < ξ 0 , where ξ 0 is the zero-temperature coherence length the effective Zeeman field is h = (J sd /d S )m. Here J sd is the interfacial exchange interaction constant 54 proportional to the spin-mixing angle 51,52 .

The SOC is generated at the S/Pt interface within the layer which thickness is smaller than that of the superconducting film d SOC < d S . Therefore we will use the interfacial SOC potential αSO = α SO d SOC . Further we focus on a particular case of Rashba SOC when the only nonzero components of the SU(2) field are A x y = -A y x = 1. Then the free energy (2) yields the DMI in the form (1) with the vectors
div.formula: d k = (d (1) k , d (2) k , d (3) k ) d (γ) k = 2α SO a m d F I ε zγk(6)
div.p: The correction to the anisotropy energy has an easy-axis form and renormalizes the anisotropy constant to K = K -α2 SO a m ψ 2 d F I d SOC . Besides that Eq.( 2) yields the change of the exchange stiffness Ã = A -a m ψ 2 d S /d F I .

For a thin FI film superconducting corrections can renormalize the exchange stiffness and anisotropy rather strongly. The general reason is that the condensation νT 2 c ∼ 10 6 erg/cm 3 is much larger than the typical magnetic anisotropy energy |K| ∼ 10 3 erg/cm 3 . Here we assume that the Fermi wavevector of the superconductor k F = 10 8 cm -1 , T c = 10 K, E F = 10 4 K. Then we get the
div.formula: a m ∼ ξ 2 0 (h/T c ) 2 νT 2 c or a m ∼ (T c /ξ 0 )(h/T c ) 2 (k F ξ 0 ) 3 (T c /E F )
div.p: which yields a m ∼ 10 -7 (h/T c ) 2 erg/cm. Provided that h ∼ 0.5T c this correction is of the same order as the characteristic value of the stiffness constant in YIG 55,56 A ∼ 10 -7 erg/cm. The effective value of magnetic stiffness (d F I /d S )A can be reduced by reducing the relative thickness of FI. This large correction provides significant change of the domain wall size 46 in FI below T c . In combination with the effect of DMI it eventually allows for the chiral ground state in the setup shown in Fig. 1. Let us estimate the change of the domain wall energy induced by DMI. Taking into account that the anisotropy energy is K = 4 • 10 3 erg/cm 3 we get the typical Bloch domain wall thickness l Bl = A/K ∼ 10 -5 cm and energy per unit length in the absence of DMI E Bl ∼ Kd F I l Bl ∼ 10 -8 erg/cm. Taking αSO ∼ 0.1 -1 [57][58][59] we get the estimation of the DMI F DM ∼ |ψ| 2 a m /(d F I l Bl ) which yields F DM ∼ (0.1/l Bl )(h/T c ) 2 erg/cm 3 provided that |ψ| ∼ 1. The DMI contribution to the domain wall energy is δE Bl ∼ 0.1d F I (h/T c ) 2 ∼ 10 -7 (h/T c ) 2 erg/cm. For h/T c ∼ 1 it is of the same order or larger than the domain wall energy without the DMI. Thus, superconductivity-controlled DMI 1 is capable of making the domain wall energy negative leading to the forma-tion of non-homogeneous magnetic ground states 34 . The back-action of the magnetic texture leads to the the spatial modulation of the order parameter amplitude -the pair density wave state.

To study the hybrid ground state combining the pair density wave and the magnetic helix we minimize numerically the energy given by F = d S F S + d F I F F I . Introducing the spherical angles for magnetization direction m x = sin θ cos χ, m y = sin θ sin χ, m z = cos θ, we write the magnetic F F I and superconducting F S energies setting χ = 0 and introducing normalized units x/l Bl , FF I = F F I /|K|, FS = F S /|K|
div.formula: FF I = (∂ x θ) 2 ± cos 2 θ (7) FS = l Bl ξ 0 2 [ãψ 2 + b 2 ψ 4 ] + c(∂ x ψ) 2 - ãm 2 ψ 2 × (8) (∂ x θ) 2 + 2α SO l Bl d S ∂ x θ + αSO l Bl d S 2 d S d SOC cos 2 θ
div.p: where upper (lower) sign corresponds to the easy plane (axis) anisotropy K > (<)0. At h = 0.5T c the numerical values are ã = β(T /T c -1), b = 0.046β, c = 0.37β, ãm = 0.008β. where β = νT 2 c ξ 2 0 /A ∼ 10. Here we assume that FI film is thin and neglect the effect of stray fields both on the superconductivity and on the magnetic energy. This provides much more preferable conditions for the nucleation of the chiral phase than in the bulk chiral magnet 34 .

To demonstrate the possibility of the chiral state formation we consider the probe function with the homogeneously rotating magnetization and ψ = const. For simplicity we put d F I = d S = d SOC = ξ 0 in Eq. (7,8). After minimizing the free energy by ψ we get F CP DW (q) = q 2 ± 1/2 -(ã -a m (α 2 SO /4 + q 2 /2 + αSO q)) 2 /2 b. It has to be compared with the two possible homogeneous configurations with F x = -ã 2 /2 b for m = ±x and F z = ±1 -(ã -ãm α2 SO /2) 2 /2 b for m = ±z. The CPDW state with necessity occurs if F CP DW (q = 0) < F x,z which is realized under the condition 3α 2 SO a m /8 -3 b/α 2 SO a m < a < α2 SO ãm /8 -2 b/α 2 SO a m . Note that this condition can be realized for the positive overall spin stiffness ∂ 2 F/∂ 2 q > 0 in contrast to cryptofferomagnetic state. In case if either F CP DW (q = 0) > F x or F CP DW (q = 0) > F z the homogeneous state can be more preferable. This condition is sufficient but not necessary for the realization of the CPDW. The energy profile F CP DW (q) is shown in Fig. 2 for the typical parameters of the system αSO = 0.8, l Bl = ξ 0 , h = 0.5T c , β = 10. One can see that although F x > F CP DW (q = 0) > F z the minimum is given by the CPDW state since F CP DW (q = q CP DW ) < F z,z .

The probe state discussed above is not exact and overestimates the energy because it neglects the variation of ψ and considers only single q. To characterise the CPDW state we numerically minimize the energy F with boundary conditions ψ(0) = ψ(L pdw ) and θ(0, L pdw ) = 0, π, where the length L pdw is the half-period of the chiral state. Note that here we assume α SO > 0 which determines the positive chirality of the domain walls [θ(L pdw ) -θ(0)]/π = 1. For α SO < 0 the chirality will be -1. Then we compare energies of the uniform states F x,z vs F CP DW = L pdw 0 dxF/L pdw . For the parameters where the ground state is the CPDW the energy difference E dw -E un becomes negative. For calculation we choose the easy-plane K > 0 and the parameters h = 0.5T c , β = 10.

In Fig. 3a we demonstrate the characteristic phase diagram of the Pt/S/FI system in the plane of (L pdw , T ). The black line shows the equilibrium chiral state halfperiod L pdw (T ). One can see that the domain walls first occur at T pdw with the zero concentration, i.e. the infinite inter-wall distance L pdw (T pdw ) = ∞. At lower temperatures T < T pdw the finite concentration of domain walls, determined by the distance between the neighbour domain walls L pdw , appears. In the direct analogy with Abrikosov vortices in type-II superconductor the concentration of domain walls is determined by the balance between the negative energy of the individual wall and the repulsion between neighbour walls. This can be seen in Fig. 3c,d which show with the red, green and blue curves the evolution of the CPDW state components θ(x), ψ(x) with the temperature approaching T pdw as marked by crosses on the black curve L pdw (T ) in the panel Fig. 3a. The scales of θ(x) and ψ(x) relative to the period or to the distance between neighbouring defects L pdw decreases with approaching T pdw . This means that the near T pdw the CPDW state consists of the almost isolated defects with the growing distance between them L pdw (T → T pdw ) → ∞.

The probe function approach shows that the formation of CPDW state is determined by the competition between DMI and the effective anisotropy energy including the corrections in Eq.8 generated by the spin supercurrents. The anisotropy favours the homogeneous state while DMI favours the CPDW one. To study this competition in detail we calculate the phase diagram as function of T, αSO by comparing the energy of CPDW state with large L pdw = 20ξ 0 with that of the homogeneous states F x,z . The result shown in Fig. 3b demonstrates the regions where the ground state is CPDW and the homogeneous one. The solid black line shows the points of the transition from |m x | = 1 to |m z | = 1 homogeneous states where the effective anisotropy vanishes F x = F z . Near this transition the effective anisotropy vanishes, so that the CPDW region is elongated along the black curve. Due to this peculiar form there appears an interval of αSO where CPDW state has a re-entrance behaviour, e.g. with the lowering T along the black dashed line in Fig. 3b. For other values of αSO one can have a single transition into CPDW, e.g. along the red dashed lines.

To summarize, we have discovered the CPDW hybrid states combining the array of chiral domain walls and the modulation of the superconducting order parameter. The CPDW state is generated by the DMI mediated by Cooper pairs which can be considered as the contribution to the energy from superconducting spin currents. The CPDW state have several distinct features as compared to the known non-homogeneous ground states. (i) The CPDW state breaks the parity symmetry having definite sign of magnetic chirality. (ii) The typical cryptoferromagnetic state has [17][18][19] the period ξ 0 while for the CPDW it is L pdw ≥ ξ 0 where ξ 0 is the Cooper pair size.

(iii) The transition to the CPDW state occurs via the penetration of defects -chiral domain walls in contrast to the second order phase transition to the cryptoferromagnetic phase 20 . (iv) In contrast to the cryptoferromagnetic state which has been observed only in the exotic compounds 18 such hybrid chiral states can be obtained in the existing FI/S structures such as YIG/Nb/Pt or EuS/Al/Pt multilayers. The CPDW state is also qualitatively different from the previously known Fulde-Ferrel-Larkin-Ovchinnikov state 60,61 and the phase-modulated superconducting state 28,29,32 because in the CPDW state the proliferation of the inhomogeneity occurs coherently in the superconducting and magnetic subsystems. been carried out within the state task of ISSP RAS with the support by RFBR grant 19-02-00466. I.V.B. and D.S.R. also acknowledge the financial support by Foundation for the Advancement of Theoretical Physics and Mathematics "BASIS".
Appendix A: General expression for spin supercurrent
The general expression for the free energy correction due to the superconductivity is 44
div.formula: F S = νπT 8 ω Tr[D( ∇ĝ) 2 -4(ω + ihσ)τ 3 ĝ -2 ∆ĝ] (A1)
div.p: where ∇k = ∇ k -i[•, A γ k σγ ]/2. The spin supercurrent in diffuse superconductor is given by
div.formula: J γ = iπνD 16 T ω Tr(σ γ ǧ ∇ǧ),(A2)
div.p: The direct calculation of the free energy (A1) variation over the SU(2) field A γ k shows the relation Eq. ( 3) is valid in the general case.

div.head: Appendix B: Ginzburg-Landau free energy expansion
div.p: To derive the free energy of Pt/S/FI system we use Usadel equation in the presence of SOC assuming that the spin-splitting of electronic bands is much smaller than the Fermi energy. It is formulated in therms if the momentum-averaged Green function (GF) ĝ which is the matrix in Nambu and spin space
where ∇k

is the SU(2)-covariant gradient and D is the diffusion coefficient which can be layerdependent. The spin-dependent gauge field Âk = A ki σi exists in the normal metal region, the gap ∆ and exchange field h are non-zero in the superconductor and ferromagnet, respectively. Close to the critical temperature we have the expansion (we consider ω > 0)
div.formula: ĝ = τ3 + δĝ (B2)
div.p: where δĝ is the expansion by ∆, ∇∆ and D k m. To begin with, we find the first-order correction by ∆
We are interested only in the spin-singlet part anomalous part of GF which is
In case if |h| = const the gradient term in Eq.B1 yields corrections to the spin-singlet part of the GF
In case if the absolute value of the exchange field is constant |h| = const the above expression can be simplified by taking into account that (D k h)h = 0, so that
div.formula: D k (hD k h) = D k hD k h + hD k D k h = 0. Then we get g ∇ = (ω 2 -h 2 )∇(D∇∆) (ω 2 + h 2 ) 2 - D∆(D k h) 2 (ω 2 + h 2 ) 2 (B6)
div.p: The self-consistency equation reads

where τ = 1 -T /T c . Introducing the dimensionless order parameter ψ = ∆/T c we get the GL equation This energy is to be compared with the energies of the two possible homogeneous stated Thus, one can see that with lowering temperature and thus decreasing a we first encounter the homogeneous magnetization rotation transition to m = ±z state and after that there is a transition to CPDW.
div.formula: aψ -a m (D k m) 2 ψ + bψ 3 -∇(c∇ψ) = 0 (B8) a/νT 2 c = πT c ω>0 h 2 ω(h 2 + ω 2 ) -τ(
F x = ãψ 2 + b 2 ψ 4(C2)

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. (Red solid line): The free energy of the CPDW probe state with the uniformly rotated magnetization in the presence of SOC αSO = 0. The equilibrium wave number is qCP DW . (Blue dashed line): The same system in the absence of SOC. (Black, green lines) : Energy levels Fx and Fz of the homogeneous states with m x and m z, respectively.The temperature is T = 0.75Tc and the other parameters are defined in the text. The plot corresponds to easy-axis anisotropy K < 0. The case although K > 0 looks very similar.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. (a) Phase diagram of Pt/S/FI system as function of (T, L pdw ) with ᾱSO = 1.5. The black curve shows equilibrium CPDW state period L pdw(T ) . (b) Phase diagram of Pt/S/FI system as a function of (T, αSO) showing the regions of single transition (e.g. along red dashed line) and the re-entrant behaviour of the CPDW state (e.g. along blue dashed lines). The solid black line shows the points of vanishing effective anisotropy where Fx = Fz. (c,d) Structure of PDW state: magnetic part θ(x) and superconducting part ψ(x). The red, green and blue lines correspond to the parameters marked by the red, green and blue crosses in the panel (a). In all panels dF I = dS = dSOC = ξ0, l Bl = 1.5ξ0, other parameters are described in the text.

figure.head: ω 2 2 0 = πT 2 c ω>0 h 2 (h 2 + 2 (D k m) 2 ψ 2 + b 2 ψ 4 +
figure.figDesc: -h 2 (h 2 + ω 2 ) 2 (B11) a m /νT 2 c ξ ω 2 ) 2 (B12)At h = 0.5T c the numerical values are ã = 0.026 -τ , b = 0.046, c = 0.37, ãm = 0.008. Since the GL equation is obtained from the condition δF S /δψ = 0 we get the expression for the free energy GL functionalF S = aψ 2 -a m cD(∇ψ) 2 (B13)Appendix C: Proof of the CPDW state existence with the help of the probe functionTo demonstrate the possibility of the chiral state formation we consider the probe function with the homogeneously rotating magnetization m x = -sin(qx), m y = cos(qx) and ψ = const. Substituting this probe function into the Eqs.7,8 in the main text and averaging this by the period of modulation 2L pdw = 2π/q we get F CP DW = q 2

figure.head: F z = 1 + 2 + q 2 F
figure.figDesc: (a -α2 SO ãm /2)ψ 2 can see that the transition from m = ±x to m = ±z state occurs at the temperature defined by F z < F x if F CP DW (q = 0) < min(F z , F x ) which yields the condition defining the temperature interval 3ã m α2

div.p: Acknowledgements This work was supported by the Academy of Finland (Project No. 297439) and Russian science foundation. MAS acknowledges useful discussions with Tero Heikkilä and Yao Lu. The work of I.V.B has
